title: Voices in the lighthouse
summary: A Norwegian woman remembers the radio voices that filled her childhood lighthouse — strangers who never came, yet stayed.
body:
My name is Aiko-Marit. My father was a lighthouse keeper. He worked on a small island in Norway. I lived there from age four to age fifteen.

There were three people on the island. My father, my mother, and me. The wind was loud all winter. The boats came every two weeks.

My father had a radio. It was big and grey. Every evening at seven, he turned it on. He listened to fishermen at sea. They spoke from their boats. They reported the weather. They reported their position. They sometimes said hello to each other.

I was a small girl. I did not know any of them. But I knew their voices.

There was Bjørn, who fished cod. There was Ingrid, who fished crab. There was a man called only 'the Captain.' His voice was very deep. He always said, 'Good evening to all good people.'

I never saw any of them. I do not know what they looked like.

When I was fifteen, we moved to Bergen. The radio stayed in the lighthouse. The new keeper kept it on at seven, my father said.

Now I am forty. I live in a city. I have a son. He has many friends online. He talks to them every day. He has not seen most of them.

My mother once said, 'You children today have invisible friends.' I laughed. I said, 'Mother, I had them too. They fished crab.'

annotations.meaningVi: người trông coi / người canh giữ
đài radio (thiết bị nhận sóng âm thanh)
ngoài biển / trên biển (cụm cố định)

grammarNotes.title: Past simple to tell a life story
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để kể lại đời mình, dùng quá khứ đơn cho hành động đã xong: 'My father was a lighthouse keeper.' 'He worked on a small island.' 'I lived there from age four to age fifteen.' 'When I was fifteen, we moved to Bergen.' Động từ thường thêm -ed (worked, lived, moved); một số bất quy tắc (was, knew, said).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use past simple to tell a life story of finished events: 'My father was a lighthouse keeper.' 'He worked on a small island.' 'I lived there from age four to age fifteen.' 'When I was fifteen, we moved to Bergen.' Regular verbs add -ed (worked, lived, moved); irregular ones have their own form (was, knew, said).

translationVi:
Tên tôi là Aiko-Marit. Cha tôi là người gác hải đăng. Ông làm việc trên một hòn đảo nhỏ ở Na Uy. Tôi sống ở đó từ năm bốn tuổi đến mười lăm tuổi.

Trên đảo có ba người. Cha tôi, mẹ tôi, và tôi. Suốt mùa đông gió ồn. Thuyền đến hai tuần một lần.

Cha tôi có một cái radio. To và xám. Mỗi tối lúc bảy giờ, cha bật lên. Ông nghe ngư dân ngoài biển. Họ nói từ thuyền của mình. Họ báo thời tiết. Họ báo vị trí. Họ đôi khi chào nhau.

Tôi là một bé gái nhỏ. Tôi không quen ai trong số họ. Nhưng tôi biết giọng họ.

Có Bjørn, đánh cá tuyết. Có Ingrid, bắt cua. Có một người chỉ gọi là 'Thuyền Trưởng.' Giọng ông rất trầm. Ông luôn nói: 'Chào buổi tối tất cả những người tốt.'

Tôi chưa từng thấy ai trong số họ. Tôi không biết họ trông ra sao.

Khi tôi mười lăm, gia đình chuyển đến Bergen. Cái radio ở lại ngọn hải đăng. Người gác mới vẫn bật lúc bảy giờ, cha tôi kể.

Giờ tôi bốn mươi. Tôi sống trong thành phố. Tôi có một con trai. Con có nhiều bạn trên mạng. Con nói chuyện với họ mỗi ngày. Con chưa gặp phần lớn họ.

Một hôm mẹ tôi nói: 'Tụi nhỏ bây giờ có bạn vô hình.' Tôi cười. Tôi đáp: 'Mẹ ơi, hồi đó con cũng có. Họ bắt cua.'

questions.prompt: How many people lived on the island?
questions.explanation: 'There were three people on the island.'

questions.prompt: At what time did her father turn on the radio?
questions.explanation: 'Every evening at seven, he turned it on.'
'They reported the weather. They reported their position.'

questions.prompt: Where did Aiko-Marit's family move when she was fifteen?
questions.explanation: 'When I was fifteen, we moved to Bergen.'

questions.prompt: What does her mother say about modern children?

options: 'Mother, I had them too. They fished crab.'
Connection at a distance is not new — every generation builds friendships with voices, just through different tools
Children should not use the internet
Lighthouses are dangerous places for children
Crab fishing is the best job
questions.explanation: The closing comparison makes this point.
questions.hint: Think about how she answers her mother.

blanks.explanation: The boats came every two weeks.
She knew their voices, not their faces.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Aiko-Marit compares her childhood radio voices to her son's online friends. Is there a 'voice without a face' that has shaped you — a podcast host, a teacher you only emailed, a stranger you only know online? What does the fact that you have never seen them change, if anything?
